summary: This writeup aggregates and analyzes top-performing approaches to the ARC Prize 2024 competition, highlighting how participants combined test-time training with LoRA-tuned LLMs, classical DSL solvers, custom transformers, and lightweight pre-processing tricks. The author emphasizes that hybrid strategies—merging symbolic reasoning with neural inference and leveraging active-inference loops—consistently outperformed pure end-to-end models. Key insights revolve around the effectiveness of reverse augmentation, colour remapping for palette-irrelevant tasks, and probabilistic ensemble voting to maximize coverage across diverse task types.
extensive_summary:
## LLM & Test-Time Training Approaches
The 2nd place solution ("Omni-ARC TTT") utilized test-time training (TTT) on a LoRA-tuned Qwen-0.5 B model, focusing on both drawing and reasoning capabilities. It introduced an AIRV loop (augment → infer → reverse → vote) that outperformed standard rotations and colour permutations. Synthetic training grids were generated to address sparse supervision, and a fallback to a 2020 C++ DSL solver handled purely symbolic cases.

## Classical Solvers & Heuristics
Several top solutions relied on hand-coded rule engines. The 3rd place approach used a library of 120 symmetry, flood-fill, and pattern functions, grid-searching them against training examples with metadata-driven heuristics. The 4th place solution expanded on the 2020 DSL/DAG search, adding CNN and decision-tree modules for colour counts and masks, alongside heavy data augmentation. The 5th place solution merged six 2020 solvers with an iterative solver, using post-filters to auto-repair GA errors and brute-forcing task order via binary search.

## Custom Architectures & Pre-processing
The 13th place solution built a 19-token custom Transformer from scratch using PyTorch 2.2, introducing a novel grid positional encoding that surpassed classical sinusoidal methods. An active-inference/reverse augmentation loop contributed significantly to its score. The 21st place solution demonstrated that a lightweight NumPy pre-processor (`recolor_task()`) could remap colours by pixel-count order, yielding a +2 point gain on single-test-pair tasks by exploiting human-like colour-irrelevance detection.

## LLM Hybrids & Competition Context
The 34th place solution fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1 8B via Unsloth, sweeping temperatures and prompts to generate candidate grids, then fused results with a classic solver. The writeup concludes by noting key differences for the 2025 iteration, including stricter submission limits, a fresh task set, and increased GPU compute allowances.
competition_flow: Raw grid tasks are processed through augmentation or colour remapping, passed to either neural models (LoRA-tuned LLMs, custom transformers) or classical solvers (DSL, flood-fill), then combined via ensemble voting or probabilistic sampling before final submission.
data_processing: Generates synthetic train grids to thicken sparse supervision
features_engineering: Library of 120 hand-coded symmetry / flood-fill / pattern functions
CNN & decision-tree modules for colour counts & masks
Task metadata heuristics to order functions
ensembling: Multiple solutions combined outputs via majority vote, probabilistic sampling proportional to unique solves, or fallback fusion with classical solvers to maximize task coverage.
insights: Active-Inference / reverse augmentation loop yields significant score gains
Grid positional encoding outperforms classical sinusoidal encoding
It is easier to delete bad answers than pick the best one, so keeping top-2 attempts is strategic
Humans spot colour-irrelevance instantly, making cheap pre-processing gains possible
critical_findings: AIRV loop beats standard rotations & colour permutations
Falling back to a 2020 C++ DSL solver for purely symbolic cases adds +14 pts
Re-color pre-processor adds +2 pts over IceCuber but only works for single-test-pair tasks
